title.zh: 令丘山上预示大旱的人面枭
title.en: Human-Faced Owl That Portends Great Drought

summary.zh: 令丘山怪鸟，形状像猫头鹰，人的面孔，四只眼睛，长着耳朵，叫声就是自己的名字；它一出现，天下大旱。
summary.en: A bird of Mount Lingqiu shaped like an owl, with a human face, four eyes, and ears, calling out its own name; wherever it appears, great drought follows across the land.

description.zh:
令丘之山寸草不生，遍地多火，山南有一道中谷，东北风（条风）从谷中吹出。《南山经》记这里的怪鸟："有鸟焉，其状如枭，人面四目而有耳，其名曰颙，其鸣自号也，见则天下大旱。"它像一只猫头鹰，却生人脸，脸上四只眼，头侧还有明显的耳朵；叫声"颙、颙"，正是自己的名字。

颙属于《山海经》征兆体系中分量最重的一类——影响所及是"天下"而非一县一郡。同样"见则天下大旱"的还有《西山经》太华山上六足四翼的肥遗蛇；而在本卷之内，长右现则郡县大水，颙现则天下大旱，水旱两端各有司职的怪物。它栖身的山本就多火无草木，仿佛旱象在地貌里先行写好。历代图本多画一只圆睁四目的人面鸮，蹲在焦土之上——在靠雨水吃饭的农耕世界，这是能让人失眠的鸟。
description.en:
Mount Lingqiu bears no vegetation and burns with constant fires; from the Middle Valley on its south side blows the northeast wind. Here lives the yong. The Classic of the Southern Mountains describes an owl-like bird with a human face set with four eyes, and ears plainly visible on its head; its call, 'yong, yong', is its own name. The closing clause carries the weight: when it is seen, great drought comes to all under heaven.

That scope places the yong in the heaviest class of the book's portents — those whose effects reach the whole world rather than a single county. Its counterpart in the west is the six-legged, four-winged serpent Feiyi of Mount Taihua, another harbinger of universal drought, while within this same volume the ape-like Changyou brings floods to whichever district it visits: water and drought each have their appointed monster. Fittingly, the yong roosts on a mountain already scorched and grassless, as if the drought were written into the landscape before the bird arrives. Illustrated editions draw a four-eyed, human-faced owl squatting on bare earth — in a world that lived by rainfall, a bird to lose sleep over.

value.en: Its appearance brings drought to all under heaven
